description: Betelgeuse is a red supergiant star in the constellation Orion, one of the largest and most luminous stars visible to the naked eye. It is a semi-regular variable star whose apparent magnitude varies between 0.0 and 1.3. At some point in the relatively near future (in astronomical terms), Betelgeuse is expected to explode as a supernova.

Sirius is the brightest star in the night sky, with an apparent magnitude of −1.46. It is a binary star system consisting of a main-sequence star of spectral type A1V and a faint white dwarf companion. It is nicknamed the 'Dog Star' due to its prominence in the constellation Canis Major.

Proxima Centauri is a small, low-mass red dwarf star located 4.243 light-years away in the southern constellation of Centaurus — the closest known star to our Sun. It hosts at least two confirmed exoplanets, including Proxima b in the habitable zone.

Vega is the brightest star in the constellation Lyra and the fifth-brightest star in the night sky. It was used as the northern pole star around 12,000 BCE and will be again around 13,727 CE. Vega was the first star other than the Sun to be photographed and to have its spectrum recorded.

Rigel is the brightest star in Orion and the seventh-brightest star in the night sky. It is a blue supergiant — extremely luminous, burning some 120,000 times brighter than our Sun. Rigel forms the left foot (or knee) of the Hunter Orion.

Antares is a massive red supergiant star in the Milky Way galaxy and the brightest star in the constellation Scorpius. Its name means 'rival to Ares (Mars)', derived from its reddish color. Antares is about 700 times larger than our Sun and is expected to explode as a supernova within the next million years.

Pollux is an orange giant star in the constellation Gemini and the brightest star in that constellation. It has a confirmed exoplanet, Pollux b (also designated Thestias), one of the nearest known exoplanets. Pollux is the closest giant star to the Sun.

Aldebaran is an orange giant star located about 65.3 light-years from the Sun in the zodiac constellation Taurus. It is the brightest star in Taurus and one of the brightest stars in the night sky. The name Aldebaran is derived from the Arabic al-Dabarān, meaning 'the follower'.

Alpha Centauri A is the largest of the three stars in the Alpha Centauri system and the closest star system to our Solar System. Together with Alpha Centauri B, it forms a binary pair, while Proxima Centauri orbits at a much greater distance. Alpha Centauri A is very similar to our Sun but slightly larger and more luminous.

Canopus is the brightest star in the constellation Carina and the second-brightest star in the night sky after Sirius. It is a white-yellow supergiant with a luminosity about 10,700 times that of the Sun. Canopus is used as a navigational beacon by spacecraft because it is so bright and in a convenient location in the sky.

Arcturus is a red giant star in the Northern Hemisphere of Earth's sky and the brightest star in the constellation Boötes. It is the fourth-brightest star in the night sky. Arcturus is relatively old and has exhausted hydrogen in its core. Its name is derived from the Ancient Greek meaning 'guardian of the bear'.

Spica is a binary star and the brightest object in the constellation Virgo. It is one of the brightest stars in the night sky, with a combined apparent magnitude of +0.97. The primary component is a blue giant and one of the nearest massive binary star systems to Earth.

Deneb is the brightest star in the constellation Cygnus and one of the vertices of the Summer Triangle asterism. Despite being one of the most intrinsically luminous stars in our galaxy, Deneb appears only moderately bright due to its enormous distance from Earth — roughly 2,600 light-years away.

Fomalhaut is the brightest star in the constellation Piscis Austrinus. It was once suspected to host an exoplanet (Fomalhaut b), though this remains controversial. Fomalhaut is surrounded by a dusty disc — a debris disk — that is one of the best-studied in the sky.

Regulus is the brightest star in the constellation Leo and one of the brightest stars in the night sky. A four-star system, its primary star is a young blue-white main-sequence star rotating so rapidly that it is noticeably oblate. Its name comes from Latin for 'little king' or 'prince'.

Castor is the second-brightest star in Gemini. It is actually a sextuple star system — three binary pairs orbiting each other. It consists of two A-class main-sequence stars and one faint red dwarf pair. Castor forms a famous naked-eye pair with Pollux (the Gemini twins).

Acrux is the brightest star in the constellation Crux (the Southern Cross) and the brightest star in the southern sky. It is actually a triple star system. Acrux appears on the flag of several Southern Hemisphere countries. It is not visible from most of the Northern Hemisphere.

Achernar is the brightest star in the constellation Eridanus and the ninth-brightest star in the night sky. It is a hot blue-white star spinning so rapidly that its equatorial diameter is more than 50% larger than its polar diameter — making it the most oblate known star.

Procyon is the brightest star in the constellation Canis Minor and the eighth-brightest star in the night sky. It is a binary star system consisting of a main-sequence star (Procyon A) and a faint white dwarf companion (Procyon B). Its name comes from the Greek for 'before the dog', as it rises just before Sirius.

Hadar (also known as Agena) is the second-brightest star in Centaurus and the tenth-brightest star in the night sky. It is a triple star system and one of the two 'pointer stars' of the Southern Cross. Hadar is a blue giant star with a high surface temperature.
